standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English capitalization, punctuation, and spelling when writing.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Choosing between its and it's
khan_exercises.description: This is one of the most common slip-ups you can make in English! Let's give it a shot.
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct form of `its`—with or without an apostrophe—for this sentence.**

The book’s summary gives away some of  \_\_\_\_\_\_  best parts!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**"It's"** and **"its"** are often mixed up:

* **"it's"** is a contraction of **"it is"** or **"it has"**
* **"its"** is a possessive pronoun that shows something *belongs* to **"it"**

If you're not sure which one to use, try inserting **"it is"** into the same place and see if it makes sense. If it does, use an apostrophe. If it doesn't, don't.

For example:

>The pot roast cooked in **it's/its** own juices.

>The pot roast cooked in $\redD{\text{it is}}$ own juices? **That doesn't make sense, so we can't use "it's".**

>The pot roast cooked in $\blueD{\text{its}}$ own juices. **Much better!**
We should use the possessive form **its**.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

For example:

>The pot roast cooked in $\redD{\text{it is}}$ own juices? **That doesn't make sense, so we can't use "it's".**

>The pot roast cooked in $\blueD{\text{its}}$ own juices. **Much better!**
We should use **it's** as a contraction of "it is".
